title: document that rootless mode enables lingering mode
body: add a note to the documentation explaining why rootless containers need lingering mode

comments.body:
Just noting here I don't think rootless podman should *automatically* try to change the host system to enable this.  Let's just print a clear error message.

The rationale for this is that while it's convenient for "user owns machine" cases, we also want to support "organization owns machine" cases where the user can't/shouldn't gain root on their systems.  For those types of scenarios, enabling linger is something that the "system administrator" should do via e.g. a config management system.

Yes, I agree with @cgwalters 

People might get surprised if using Toolbox toggles the lingering mode of the system because they think of `toolbox enter` as a shell, which it basically is.
I agree we should not do this by default.  But documenting it does not hurt.  We want to tell the admin how to enable it.

since it seems there is agreement on disabling lingering mode by default, I've opened a PR here:

https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/4269

I believe that it should be disabled by default, but add a flag to podman create/run that allows users to specify --linger and have the container linger if the admin allows the user to linger processes.
[This issue](https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/40708) is the reason. Just one docker developer made wrong decision and broke rootless mode.
@andrew-aladev Even if we avoided using XDG dirs, anyway you need to use linger mode to keep user processes running on systemd hosts.

@AkihiroSuda, `$HOME/.local/share` or `${HOME}/.cache` ar good standard pathes stable across sessions. `XDG_*` path is designed for software that don't want to store some data across sessions. These directories has been implemented as re-mounted tmpfs endpoiints.
